question_text: What is the administrator focus when a resident has dementia-related memory loss?
answer_a: Organizing care around current abilities, preferences, safety needs, staff direction, and documentation.
answer_b: Diagnosing the dementia type before staff may provide support.
answer_c: Replacing the service plan with informal caregiver preferences.
answer_d: Waiting for the resident to request every needed support independently.
rationale: The course frames dementia care through RCFE operational oversight: individualized support, staff consistency, privacy, and objective records rather than diagnosis.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Which statement best describes person-centered dementia care in an RCFE?
answer_a: A standard script used with every resident who has memory loss.
answer_b: A way to avoid documenting distress if staff used a kind tone.
answer_c: Care that uses the resident's history, routines, abilities, and preferences to guide support.
answer_d: A family-directed plan that overrides resident privacy and current observations.
rationale: Person-centered care connects the resident's known life patterns and current needs to practical support while preserving rights and dignity.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Why should staff avoid labeling a resident as difficult during care?
answer_a: Because all dementia-related behavior must be ignored unless injury occurs.
answer_b: Because labels hide observable facts, possible unmet needs, and useful care-planning information.
answer_c: Because only family members may describe resident behavior.
answer_d: Because documentation should include staff opinions before observations.
rationale: Judgmental labels reduce the quality of the record and can steer staff away from identifying triggers, needs, and effective approaches.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Which change should prompt timely review rather than being dismissed as routine dementia?
answer_a: A resident preferring tea with breakfast.
answer_b: A resident enjoying familiar music during activities.
answer_c: A resident asking the same question twice in one afternoon.
answer_d: A sudden change in confusion, mobility, intake, sleep, mood, or behavior pattern.
rationale: The course emphasizes escalation and follow-up for meaningful or sudden changes because new concerns can be missed when every change is attributed to dementia.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: What is a useful first documentation habit when a resident resists personal care?
answer_a: Record the observable behavior, context, staff approach, resident response, and follow-up need.
answer_b: Record that the resident was non-compliant and close the note.
answer_c: Skip documentation if the resident later accepts care.
answer_d: Ask another resident whether the behavior is common.
rationale: Objective documentation helps the administrator review patterns, coach staff, update the plan, and support continuity across shifts.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: What does it mean to interpret behavior as communication?
answer_a: Assume the resident is choosing to challenge staff authority.
answer_b: Look for possible needs, discomfort, fear, routine mismatch, or environmental triggers behind the behavior.
answer_c: Use the same redirection activity for every resident.
answer_d: Stop all care tasks whenever a resident uses a loud voice.
rationale: Behavior may reflect needs the resident cannot express clearly; administrators guide staff to investigate context before judging intent.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Which staff approach is most likely to support a resident who has difficulty processing instructions?
answer_a: Give several instructions at once so the resident knows the full plan.
answer_b: Stand behind the resident and begin the task quickly.
answer_c: Ask a long open-ended question during a noisy shift change.
answer_d: Approach calmly from the front, use the preferred name, and offer one step at a time.
rationale: Simple cues, respectful approach, and reduced processing demands are more supportive than rushed or complex communication.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Why can family input be useful in dementia-care planning?
answer_a: It allows families to approve staff schedules.
answer_b: It replaces the administrator's need to document observations.
answer_c: It may identify routines, preferences, calming phrases, and history that support individualized care.
answer_d: It permits unrestricted sharing of resident information with any relative.
rationale: Family input can enrich the plan when handled through appropriate privacy boundaries and paired with current resident observations.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: What is the best reason to include environmental review in a dementia-care response?
answer_a: Lighting, noise, temperature, clutter, signage, and activity level can increase or reduce distress.
answer_b: The environment matters only after a medication change is ordered.
answer_c: Environmental details are outside administrator oversight.
answer_d: Residents with dementia respond identically to every setting.
rationale: The course links environmental conditions to behavior patterns and administrator responsibility for practical adjustments.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: What should the administrator avoid when documenting dementia-related distress?
answer_a: Including time of day and staff approach.
answer_b: Naming the intervention attempted.
answer_c: Recording the resident's response to a calm cue.
answer_d: Using blame-based conclusions instead of observable facts.
rationale: Blame-based wording weakens dignity and makes it harder to identify patterns, triggers, and person-centered interventions.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Which phrase is the most objective documentation wording?
answer_a: Resident was impossible during shower time.
answer_b: Resident pulled towel to chest and said leave me alone when staff approached with shower supplies.
answer_c: Resident was attention-seeking again.
answer_d: Resident refused because of a bad attitude.
rationale: The objective wording describes what was seen and heard without assigning motive or blame.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: What is the main purpose of staff coaching in this course?
answer_a: To make staff use identical words in every resident interaction.
answer_b: To replace service planning with informal reminders.
answer_c: To turn observations into respectful, consistent, documented care actions.
answer_d: To keep family contacts from contributing preference information.
rationale: Coaching helps staff apply person-centered practices consistently and document useful information for plan review.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: A staff note says, Resident is combative with dressing every morning. What administrator response is most appropriate?
answer_a: Ask staff to describe observable actions, timing, approach used, likely discomfort, and what helped or worsened the interaction.
answer_b: Accept the note because it clearly describes the resident's personality.
answer_c: Tell staff not to write anything unless the resident is injured.
answer_d: Call the behavior intentional unless family says otherwise.
rationale: The administrator should coach staff toward facts and patterns so the plan can address possible needs without blaming the resident.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: A resident repeatedly asks to go home after dinner. Which response best applies validation?
answer_a: You live here now, so stop asking.
answer_b: You sound worried about home; let's sit together and have some tea while we look at your evening routine.
answer_c: Everyone has already answered that question today.
answer_d: If you ask again, we will document refusal.
rationale: Validation acknowledges the emotion and redirects toward comfort without arguing or escalating distress.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Staff disagree about whether a resident is refusing care or frightened. What should the administrator do next?
answer_a: Let each staff member decide independently by shift.
answer_b: Remove the care task from the plan permanently.
answer_c: Tell staff the resident is refusing unless the resident says the word fear.
answer_d: Review patterns, staff approach, environment, possible unmet needs, and documentation before updating staff instructions.
rationale: Administrator oversight requires a fact-based review that can support plan changes and consistent staff direction.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Which service-plan instruction is most usable for staff?
answer_a: Provide dementia care as needed.
answer_b: Be patient with the resident.
answer_c: Support the resident's evening routine by approaching from the front, offering two clothing choices, warming the room, and re-approaching after ten minutes if safe.
answer_d: Handle behaviors appropriately and tell the administrator later.
rationale: Specific plan language is more actionable than broad reminders and helps staff repeat the approach that protects dignity.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: A resident becomes agitated in a loud dining room but eats well in a quieter area. What administrator action fits the course content?
answer_a: Review whether the dining environment should be adjusted in the plan and document the resident's response.
answer_b: Tell staff the resident must adapt to the standard dining arrangement.
answer_c: Stop documenting meals because intake improved elsewhere.
answer_d: Ask family to solve the dining-room issue privately.
rationale: The observation suggests an environmental support that should be considered for individualized planning and documented follow-up.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: A staff member says, He is manipulating us with the call light. What coaching response best fits person-centered practice?
answer_a: Agree if the call light use is frequent.
answer_b: Remove the call light during busy periods.
answer_c: Tell staff to answer only every third call.
answer_d: Ask staff to review timing, need, discomfort, loneliness, confusion, and response patterns before drawing conclusions.
rationale: The coaching response redirects staff from motive-based language to unmet-need review and objective observation.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: When should the administrator consider updating dementia-care plan instructions?
answer_a: Only after a licensing visit requests it.
answer_b: When patterns, preferences, risks, staff observations, or resident responses show the current approach needs adjustment.
answer_c: Only when family members write the new plan language.
answer_d: Never, because dementia plans should stay fixed for consistency.
rationale: Plans should remain current as needs, responses, and risks change; consistency depends on updated shared instructions.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Which family communication is most appropriate after a fictional resident shows repeated evening distress?
answer_a: Your family member is causing problems after dinner.
answer_b: We can promise the new approach will stop all distress.
answer_c: We observed evening distress and are adjusting privacy, pacing, and comfort; do you know any familiar evening routines that may help?
answer_d: Send the full staff disagreement thread to every relative.
rationale: The appropriate message is factual, privacy-aware, and invites relevant preference information without blaming or overpromising.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: A resident resists morning bathing but accepts a sponge bath after breakfast. What should the administrator emphasize?
answer_a: Document the successful timing and approach, review preference patterns, and consider plan language for future care.
answer_b: Require the original schedule because the task was assigned for morning.
answer_c: Tell staff to avoid bathing documentation when the outcome is acceptable.
answer_d: Assume the resident no longer needs hygiene support.
rationale: The accepted alternative provides useful planning information that should be documented and converted into consistent care direction.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: A caregiver uses a calm tone but asks five questions at once. What coaching point should the administrator make?
answer_a: Tone is the only communication factor that matters.
answer_b: Use one-step cues and allow time to process before repeating or adding choices.
answer_c: Ask more questions so the resident can practice memory skills.
answer_d: Stop offering choices in all dementia-care situations.
rationale: Calm tone helps, but too much information can overwhelm a resident who has difficulty processing language.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Which response best protects privacy during a practice assignment?
answer_a: Use a recent real resident event but remove the last name.
answer_b: Use the facility name but change the resident's age.
answer_c: Use only the fictional case facts or neutral invented details that do not identify anyone.
answer_d: Upload a copied care note if the learner thinks it is helpful.
rationale: The course requires fictional or neutral details so learners can practice without exposing protected or identifying information.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: What should a documentation note include after staff coaching is completed?
answer_a: Only the phrase staff coached.
answer_b: A statement that all future behavior has been resolved.
answer_c: A list of staff opinions about why the resident acted that way.
answer_d: The observed issue, coaching provided, plan update if any, resident response, and follow-up need.
rationale: A useful note shows what was observed, what direction was given, how the plan changed, and what still needs monitoring.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Scenario: A resident walks toward the exit each day at 4 p.m. and says work is waiting. What is the best administrator direction?
answer_a: Review the pattern, former routine, activity schedule, environmental cues, safety risk, and person-centered redirection plan.
answer_b: Tell staff to argue that the resident is retired.
answer_c: Ignore the pattern if the resident has not left the building.
answer_d: Document the resident as intentionally eloping without further review.
rationale: The scenario calls for pattern review and individualized support because the time and statement may reflect former role, routine, or unmet need.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Scenario: Staff report that a resident hits during sweater changes. What question should the administrator prioritize?
answer_a: Which staff member can complete the task fastest?
answer_b: Can we skip all clothing changes permanently?
answer_c: What exactly happens before the hitting, and could pain, temperature, privacy, or approach be contributing?
answer_d: Can the family tell the resident to behave better?
rationale: The administrator needs observable sequence and possible contributors before staff can adjust the approach or escalate concerns appropriately.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Scenario: A family contact asks for details about another resident's behavior in the dining room. How should staff be directed?
answer_a: Share details if the family contact is worried.
answer_b: Provide names only if the event was minor.
answer_c: Ask the family contact to keep the information confidential.
answer_d: Protect privacy and discuss only the resident-specific information the contact is authorized to receive.
rationale: Dementia-care communication must preserve privacy even when families have understandable concerns.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Scenario: A resident calms when staff play familiar music before grooming. What is the best plan-related action?
answer_a: Use the music approach for every resident with dementia.
answer_b: Document the response and consider adding the individualized music cue to the grooming routine instructions.
answer_c: Stop observing because the issue is solved forever.
answer_d: Tell staff the music replaces all other support needs.
rationale: A successful individualized cue should be documented and considered for plan language while monitoring whether it continues to help.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Scenario: During a noisy shift change, a resident refuses medication assistance from authorized staff. What should the administrator review?
answer_a: Whether timing, noise, staff approach, privacy, discomfort, or routine disruption affected the resident's response.
answer_b: Whether the resident should be labeled non-compliant in all future notes.
answer_c: Whether staff can omit documentation because the hallway was busy.
answer_d: Whether family can be asked to administer medications instead.
rationale: The scenario points to environmental and approach factors that may affect cooperation and require appropriate documentation and follow-up.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Scenario: A resident with dementia has a sudden increase in confusion and reduced intake. What administrator response fits the course?
answer_a: Assume progression and wait for the next routine review.
answer_b: Ask staff to keep the change informal until family visits.
answer_c: Remove the resident from activities without documenting why.
answer_d: Ensure timely reporting through facility channels, document observations, and follow escalation procedures.
rationale: Sudden changes require timely attention and should not be dismissed as expected dementia behavior.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Scenario: Staff write that a resident is attention-seeking because she calls out after lunch. Which administrator follow-up is best?
answer_a: Tell staff not to respond unless the resident uses the call light.
answer_b: Ask whether the resident is allowed to call out under facility policy.
answer_c: Review pain, toileting, fatigue, loneliness, overstimulation, and what responses reduce distress.
answer_d: Ask another resident to reassure her after lunch.
rationale: The administrator should convert the label into a structured unmet-need review and staff response plan.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Scenario: A resident is more cooperative with a familiar caregiver. What should the administrator avoid?
answer_a: Reviewing introduction routines for unfamiliar staff.
answer_b: Assuming only that caregiver can provide care and leaving the plan undocumented.
answer_c: Documenting which approach works well.
answer_d: Coaching other staff on the successful approach.
rationale: Depending on one caregiver without plan language creates inconsistency and weakens continuity across shifts.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Scenario: A learner wants to submit a real care note with names removed for the assignment. What is the correct course response?
answer_a: Allow it because names are removed.
answer_b: Allow it if the learner says the facility approved verbally.
answer_c: Allow it only if the resident has dementia.
answer_d: Reject it and require the fictional case or neutral invented details with no protected or identifying information.
rationale: The course privacy rule prohibits real resident or facility information in learner submissions, even when some identifiers are removed.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Scenario: A resident refuses lunch in the main dining room but eats snacks in a quiet corner. What is the best administrator action?
answer_a: Document the pattern, review environmental factors, and consider individualized mealtime supports.
answer_b: Record that the resident refuses meals and take no further action.
answer_c: Tell staff to stop offering quiet alternatives.
answer_d: Assume family preferences are the only explanation.
rationale: The mealtime pattern may reveal a support need related to stimulation, setting, or routine and should inform planning.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Scenario: Staff want a quick script for a resident who asks for a deceased spouse. What should the administrator provide?
answer_a: A rule that staff must state the death every time.
answer_b: A direction to ignore the question until it stops.
answer_c: Guidance to validate the emotion, use known comforting routines, and document the response.
answer_d: Permission to tell any story that ends the conversation fastest.
rationale: The course supports validation, individualized comfort, and documentation rather than repeated correction or unbounded improvisation.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Scenario: A staff member says a resident is always worse on bath days. What information is most useful for the administrator?
answer_a: Whether staff dislike bath assignments.
answer_b: The specific timing, room conditions, words used, privacy supports, resident cues, and response to re-approach.
answer_c: Whether the resident can be persuaded by another resident.
answer_d: How many times the staff member has complained.
rationale: Useful administrator review depends on concrete details that can become plan updates and coaching points.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Scenario: The post-test is passed, but the learner skipped required scenarios and the course is still pending approval metadata. What should happen?
answer_a: Release the certificate because the quiz score is the main evidence.
answer_b: Release the certificate if the learner promises to finish later.
answer_c: Mark the learner complete manually to avoid delay.
answer_d: Keep completion or certificate release blocked until all required activities and metadata gates are satisfied.
rationale: The completion gate requires activity evidence, assessment, participation, evaluation when required, and approval metadata before certificate release.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Scenario: Staff solved evening distress by warming the room and returning later. What documentation is most complete?
answer_a: Resident eventually complied.
answer_b: Staff did a good job and no plan review is needed.
answer_c: Resident showed distress during evening care; staff warmed room, offered privacy, re-approached later, resident accepted care, plan update and follow-up were noted.
answer_d: Resident was difficult but manageable.
rationale: The complete note links observation, intervention, resident response, plan review, and follow-up in objective language.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Scenario: A family contact offers a familiar evening routine that may calm the resident. How should the administrator use it?
answer_a: Promise the contact the routine will prevent all distress.
answer_b: Evaluate whether the routine fits current needs, add practical plan language if appropriate, coach staff, and monitor response.
answer_c: Tell staff to use it without documenting the change.
answer_d: Reject family input because only staff observations matter.
rationale: Family information can support individualized planning when it is reviewed, documented, communicated to staff, and monitored.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias

question_text: Scenario: Staff ask whether they may use the resident's real story in class because it teaches the lesson well. What is the correct answer?
answer_a: No. Use fictional or neutral details only, and keep real resident, facility, and protected information out of the course.
answer_b: Yes, if the story is educational.
answer_c: Yes, if the resident's diagnosis is common.
answer_d: Yes, if the instructor deletes the resident's room number.
rationale: The course requires privacy-safe practice materials; educational value does not justify exposing real protected or identifying details.
core_category: Managing Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias
